title: Dermatomyositis / Polymyositis
oneLiner: Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies causing proximal muscle weakness; dermatomyositis (DM) also has characteristic skin rash; both associate with systemic complications and malignancy (especially DM).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Myocarditis / arrhythmia:** Suspect if chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or ECG shows new conduction block (e.g., heart block, ventricular arrhythmia) — check troponin and urgent echo; high risk of sudden death.
- **Respiratory failure (acute interstitial lung disease or aspiration):** Suspect if tachypnoea, hypoxia, crackles on auscultation, or inability to swallow saliva (dysphagia with risk of aspiration) — need urgent CT chest, ABG, and respiratory review; may require CPAP/ventilation.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Symmetrical, proximal muscle weakness (shoulders, hips, neck flexors) — difficulty lifting arms, climbing stairs, rising from chair (Gowers’ sign).
  - Myalgia (less common than weakness).
  - Skin rash (in DM only): heliotrope rash (purple periorbital oedema), Gottron’s papules (over knuckles), shawl sign (sun-exposed V-neck), mechanic’s hands (cracked, hyperkeratotic fingers).
  - Dysphagia (pharyngeal/oesophageal involvement) — can be life-threatening.
  - Arthralgia, Raynaud’s phenomenon, fever, malaise, weight loss.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - MRC grading of proximal strength (e.g., deltoid, iliopsoas <4/5 bilaterally).
  - Neck flexor weakness (patient cannot lift head off pillow).
  - Muscle tenderness (not always present).
  - Skin: purple heliotrope, flat-topped violaceous papules over extensor surfaces of knuckles (Gottron’s), periungual erythema, telangiectasias.
  - Assess for dysphagia: ask to swallow water, observe for cough/choke.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Statin-induced myopathy:** onset after starting/modifying statin; normal or mildly elevated CK (usually <10x ULN); resolves on stopping drug.
- **Inclusion body myositis (IBM):** older onset, asymmetrical, distal weakness (e.g., finger flexors, quadriceps), slower progression; no rash; responds poorly to immuno-suppression.
- **Thyroid myopathy (hypo- or hyperthyroid):** check TSH/T4; often symmetrical proximal weakness, but no rash or systemic features.
- **Drug-induced (e.g., antimalarials, colchicine, antiretrovirals):** temporal link; normal autoantibodies.
- **Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR):** older >50, bilateral shoulder/hip pain + stiffness, preserved strength, elevated ESR/CRP; no CK elevation.
- **Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE):** can have myositis, but also malar rash, photosensitivity, serositis, positive anti-dsDNA.
- **Rhabdomyolysis:** acute, severe muscle pain, weakness, dark urine, CK >10,000 U/L; usually from trauma, toxins, infection.
- **Muscular dystrophies (e.g., Becker, limb-girdle):** family history, slower progression, early contractures; no inflammatory features on biopsy.
- **Splitter to separate DM from other myositides:** Rash (heliotrope + Gottron’s) equals DM; no rash equals PM (or IBM). If rash absent but very high CK, consider PM; but beware — PM is a diagnosis of exclusion.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 
  - Urine dipstick for blood (myoglobinuria in rhabdomyolysis).
  - ECG (look for conduction blocks, arrhythmias).
  - Swallow assessment: bedside water swallow test (if fails, urgent SALT review).
- **Bloods:**
  - **CK** (elevated in active myositis; often 10-50x ULN; can be normal in DM)
  - **Aldolase, LDH, AST, ALT** (non-specific, but can be elevated)
  - **ESR, CRP** (may be normal or mildly raised)
  - **Autoantibodies:**
    - ANA (positive in 70-80%)
    - Anti-Jo-1 (anti-tRNA synthetase) — associated with interstitial lung disease, mechanic’s hands, Raynaud’s
    - Anti-Mi-2 (DM-specific, better prognosis)
    - Anti-SRP (severe necrotising myopathy)
    - Anti-PL-7, PL-12 (other synthetases)
    - Anti-TIF1γ, anti-NXP2, anti-SAE (DM with association to malignancy — higher risk)
  - **Troponin** (if cardiac involvement suspected)
  - **Thyroid function** (to rule out thyroid myopathy)
- **Imaging:**
  - **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** (tumour screen, especially in DM — ovary, lung, breast, colon)
  - **MRI thighs** (best imaging to detect muscle oedema/inflammation — guide biopsy site)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Electromyography (EMG):** shows myopathic units + spontaneous fibrillations/positive sharp waves (not diagnostic alone, but supportive)
  - **Muscle biopsy** (gold standard): site guided by MRI; shows perifascicular atrophy (DM), endomysial inflammation (PM), fibre necrosis, perivascular inflammation
  - **Pulmonary function tests** (spirometry, gas transfer) if respiratory symptoms or anti-Jo-1 positive
  - **Swallow videofluoroscopy** if dysphagia suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Bohan and Peter criteria (1975):** proximal weakness, elevated CK, EMG findings, muscle biopsy, rash (DM only) — probable/definite ≥3 criteria. (Still used in practice but superseded by EULAR/ACR)
  - **EULAR/ACR 2017 classification criteria:** includes clinical features, antibodies, biopsy; score ≥55% (probable PM/DM). Less relevant for bedside but good for research.
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** — to screen for occult malignancy (especially in DM >40 years); omission risks missing a treatable cancer.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway, Breathing, Circulation:** if stridor or drooling (severe dysphagia) — call anaesthetics, prepare for intubation
  - If hypoxia or suspected ILD: oxygen to target SpO2 94-98%, CT chest, ABG, respiratory consult
  - If cardiac symptoms: ECG, troponin, echo, cardiology consult
  - If severe weakness or rhabdomyolysis (CK >10,000): IV fluids (0.9% NaCl, start at 200 mL/h; adjust to urine output >100 mL/h); monitor renal function
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **High-dose corticosteroids:** prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg/day) orally or IV methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily for 3 days if severe
  - **Start PPI** (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) for steroid gastroprotection
  - **Calcium + vitamin D** supplementation (if on steroids >3 months)
  - **Monitor for steroid-induced hyperglycaemia** (check capillary glucose daily)
  - **Physiotherapy** (gentle range of motion, later strengthening)
  - **Speech and language therapy** for dysphagia assessment and modified diet (e.g., soft/pureed)
  - **Screen for malignancy:** CT CAP, mammogram (if female), pelvic ultrasound (ovarian), colonoscopy if >50 or concerning symptoms
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Taper steroids** slowly (by 5 mg every 2 weeks after initial response; aim for lowest maintenance)
  - **Add steroid-sparing agent:** methotrexate (first-line, provided no ILD), azathioprine, mycophenolate, or cyclophosphamide (for severe, ILD, cardiac)
  - **IVIG** (2 g/kg over 2-5 days monthly) for refractory cases or severe dysphagia
  - **Rituximab** (off-label use) for refractory disease
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 125-150 mL/h if not NBM; adjust for rhabdomyolysis
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless bleeding risk high (consider risk vs benefit)
  - **Diet:** soft/pureed if any dysphagia; NBM if severe + SALT review
  - **Observations:** baseline then 4-hourly if stable; 1-2 hourly if on high-dose IV steroids or critically unwell; monitor for hyperglycaemia
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Prednisolone:** 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO (usually 40-60 mg daily) for 4-6 weeks, then taper. IV methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg daily for 3 days if severe (e.g., ILD, dysphagia, respiratory failure).
- **Methotrexate:** start 7.5-15 mg PO/SC weekly, increase by 2.5-5 mg every 2 weeks to target 20-25 mg weekly (max 25 mg/wk); give folic acid 5 mg weekly (24h after MTX). **Check: baseline LFTs, renal function, FBC; avoid if significant ILD.**
- **Azathioprine:** 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (starting 50 mg daily, titrate up); screen for TPMT deficiency before use.
- **Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF):** 1-2 g/day in two divided doses (for moderate-to-severe disease, especially with ILD).
- **Cyclophosphamide:** IV pulsed (e.g., 500-750 mg/m² monthly) for severe ILD or life-threatening disease; must give with mesna and aggressive hydration.
- **IVIG:** 2 g/kg divided over 2-5 days monthly (infuse slowly; premedicate with antihistamines/paracetamol if needed).
- **Rituximab:** 1000 mg IV on days 1 and 15 (off-label; with pre-medication).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Methotrexate:** avoid with cotrimoxazole (increases myelotoxicity), NSAIDs (increase MTX toxicity), avoid in significant ILD, cirrhosis, uncontrolled infection
  - **Azathioprine:** avoid with allopurinol (life-threatening toxicity) — reduce azathioprine dose by 66-75% if must use allopurinol
  - **Mycophenolate:** contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic); effective contraception needed
  - **Corticosteroids:** increase risk of infection, hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, glaucoma, psychosis; taper slowly to avoid adrenal insufficiency
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Methotrexate:** contraindicated if eGFR <30; reduce dose if mild-moderate renal impairment (requires specialist advice)
  - **Azathioprine:** no major adjustment needed unless severe hepatic impairment
  - **Mycophenolate:** no adjustment needed for renal impairment
  - **Cyclophosphamide:** reduce dose in renal impairment; avoid if eGFR <20
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prednisolone:** abrupt withdrawal causing adrenal crisis — always taper (e.g., reduce by 5 mg every 2 weeks once on ≤20 mg/day; slower when ≤10 mg/day)
  - **Methotrexate:** prescribing daily instead of weekly — leads to severe myelosuppression, mucositis, hepatotoxicity; **always confirm weekly dose**
  - **Azathioprine:** starting at full dose without checking TPMT phenotype — risk of fatal myelotoxicity
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Aspiration pneumonia** from dysphagia
  - **Acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure** due to aggressive interstitial lung disease (especially anti-Jo-1)
  - **Myocarditis** (arrhythmia, heart failure)
  - **Rhabdomyolysis** (acute kidney injury)
  - **Steroid-induced:** psychosis, hyperglycaemia, upper GI bleed, fluid overload
- **Chronic:**
  - **Calcinosis cutis** (especially in juvenile DM; can ulcerate)
  - **Contractures** from prolonged weakness
  - **Malignancy** (DM more than PM; risk highest in first 3-5 years)
  - **Lipodystrophy** (associated with juvenile DM)
  - **Osteoporosis** from chronic steroids
  - **Persistent weakness** despite immunosuppression (up to 30% have incomplete recovery)
sections.Prognosis:
- 5-year survival ~80-90% in idiopathic cases; poorer if malignancy (5-year survival ~50%), severe ILD, or cardiac involvement.
- Most patients improve with steroids, but relapses are common (30-50%); chronic immunosuppression often needed for years.
- Early detection and treatment of malignancy improves survival.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always screen for occult malignancy in DM** (CT CAP, mammogram, pelvic ultrasound, consider colonoscopy) — this is the highest-yield intervention.
- **Check PFTs and HRCT chest** in every patient with anti-Jo-1 or respiratory symptoms — ILD can progress rapidly.
- **Document baseline MRC sum score and swallow status** on admission — objective serial measurements guide treatment response.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start steroids before checking CK, autoantibodies, and arranging muscle biopsy** (unless life-threatening) — steroids alter biopsy findings and make diagnosis impossible.
- **Do NOT use NSAIDs for myalgia** (risk of acute kidney injury in rhabdomyolysis; also interaction with methotrexate).
- **Do NOT forget PPI and calcium/vitamin D** when prescribing high-dose steroids.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **New or worsening dysphagia** (cannot swallow saliva, drooling, coughing after eating)
  - **Shortness of breath** or new cough/fever (possible ILD or pneumonia)
  - **Muscle weakness progressing** (cannot get out of chair, tripping)
  - **New skin rash** (heliotrope, Gottron’s)
  - **Signs of infection:** fever, rigors, severe headache (especially on immunosuppression)
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Rheumatology clinic** within 2 weeks
  - **If malignancy screen pending:** oncology referral if positive; otherwise repeat screening in 6-12 months (per local guidelines)
  - **Repeat autoantibodies and CK** in 4-6 weeks to monitor response
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <90% on air, systolic BP <90) or any single parameter >3
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L**
  - **GCS ≤14** (or any drop)
  - **Acute dysphagia** (patient cannot manage own secretions — risk of aspiration)
  - **ECG shows new heart block, ventricular arrhythmia, or troponin rise**
  - **CK >10,000 U/L** with oliguria or AKI
  - **Worsening respiratory function** (fall in SpO2 >3% from baseline, need for oxygen)
- **Call ICU/anaesthetics if:**
  - **Stridor, stridor at rest, or inability to protect airway**
  - **Severe hypoxia (SpO2 <90% despite high-flow oxygen)**
  - **Cardiac arrest (obviously)**
  - **Need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation**

rawMarkdown:
## Dermatomyositis / Polymyositis
**One-liner:** Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies causing proximal muscle weakness; dermatomyositis (DM) also has characteristic skin rash; both associate with systemic complications and malignancy (especially DM).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Myocarditis / arrhythmia:** Suspect if chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or ECG shows new conduction block (e.g., heart block, ventricular arrhythmia) — check troponin and urgent echo; high risk of sudden death.
- **Respiratory failure (acute interstitial lung disease or aspiration):** Suspect if tachypnoea, hypoxia, crackles on auscultation, or inability to swallow saliva (dysphagia with risk of aspiration) — need urgent CT chest, ABG, and respiratory review; may require CPAP/ventilation.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Symmetrical, proximal muscle weakness (shoulders, hips, neck flexors) — difficulty lifting arms, climbing stairs, rising from chair (Gowers’ sign).
  - Myalgia (less common than weakness).
  - Skin rash (in DM only): heliotrope rash (purple periorbital oedema), Gottron’s papules (over knuckles), shawl sign (sun-exposed V-neck), mechanic’s hands (cracked, hyperkeratotic fingers).
  - Dysphagia (pharyngeal/oesophageal involvement) — can be life-threatening.
  - Arthralgia, Raynaud’s phenomenon, fever, malaise, weight loss.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - MRC grading of proximal strength (e.g., deltoid, iliopsoas <4/5 bilaterally).
  - Neck flexor weakness (patient cannot lift head off pillow).
  - Muscle tenderness (not always present).
  - Skin: purple heliotrope, flat-topped violaceous papules over extensor surfaces of knuckles (Gottron’s), periungual erythema, telangiectasias.
  - Assess for dysphagia: ask to swallow water, observe for cough/choke.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Statin-induced myopathy:** onset after starting/modifying statin; normal or mildly elevated CK (usually <10x ULN); resolves on stopping drug.
- **Inclusion body myositis (IBM):** older onset, asymmetrical, distal weakness (e.g., finger flexors, quadriceps), slower progression; no rash; responds poorly to immuno-suppression.
- **Thyroid myopathy (hypo- or hyperthyroid):** check TSH/T4; often symmetrical proximal weakness, but no rash or systemic features.
- **Drug-induced (e.g., antimalarials, colchicine, antiretrovirals):** temporal link; normal autoantibodies.
- **Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR):** older >50, bilateral shoulder/hip pain + stiffness, preserved strength, elevated ESR/CRP; no CK elevation.
- **Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE):** can have myositis, but also malar rash, photosensitivity, serositis, positive anti-dsDNA.
- **Rhabdomyolysis:** acute, severe muscle pain, weakness, dark urine, CK >10,000 U/L; usually from trauma, toxins, infection.
- **Muscular dystrophies (e.g., Becker, limb-girdle):** family history, slower progression, early contractures; no inflammatory features on biopsy.
- **Splitter to separate DM from other myositides:** Rash (heliotrope + Gottron’s) equals DM; no rash equals PM (or IBM). If rash absent but very high CK, consider PM; but beware — PM is a diagnosis of exclusion.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 
  - Urine dipstick for blood (myoglobinuria in rhabdomyolysis).
  - ECG (look for conduction blocks, arrhythmias).
  - Swallow assessment: bedside water swallow test (if fails, urgent SALT review).
- **Bloods:**
  - **CK** (elevated in active myositis; often 10-50x ULN; can be normal in DM)
  - **Aldolase, LDH, AST, ALT** (non-specific, but can be elevated)
  - **ESR, CRP** (may be normal or mildly raised)
  - **Autoantibodies:**
    - ANA (positive in 70-80%)
    - Anti-Jo-1 (anti-tRNA synthetase) — associated with interstitial lung disease, mechanic’s hands, Raynaud’s
    - Anti-Mi-2 (DM-specific, better prognosis)
    - Anti-SRP (severe necrotising myopathy)
    - Anti-PL-7, PL-12 (other synthetases)
    - Anti-TIF1γ, anti-NXP2, anti-SAE (DM with association to malignancy — higher risk)
  - **Troponin** (if cardiac involvement suspected)
  - **Thyroid function** (to rule out thyroid myopathy)
- **Imaging:**
  - **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** (tumour screen, especially in DM — ovary, lung, breast, colon)
  - **MRI thighs** (best imaging to detect muscle oedema/inflammation — guide biopsy site)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Electromyography (EMG):** shows myopathic units + spontaneous fibrillations/positive sharp waves (not diagnostic alone, but supportive)
  - **Muscle biopsy** (gold standard): site guided by MRI; shows perifascicular atrophy (DM), endomysial inflammation (PM), fibre necrosis, perivascular inflammation
  - **Pulmonary function tests** (spirometry, gas transfer) if respiratory symptoms or anti-Jo-1 positive
  - **Swallow videofluoroscopy** if dysphagia suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Bohan and Peter criteria (1975):** proximal weakness, elevated CK, EMG findings, muscle biopsy, rash (DM only) — probable/definite ≥3 criteria. (Still used in practice but superseded by EULAR/ACR)
  - **EULAR/ACR 2017 classification criteria:** includes clinical features, antibodies, biopsy; score ≥55% (probable PM/DM). Less relevant for bedside but good for research.
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** — to screen for occult malignancy (especially in DM >40 years); omission risks missing a treatable cancer.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway, Breathing, Circulation:** if stridor or drooling (severe dysphagia) — call anaesthetics, prepare for intubation
  - If hypoxia or suspected ILD: oxygen to target SpO2 94-98%, CT chest, ABG, respiratory consult
  - If cardiac symptoms: ECG, troponin, echo, cardiology consult
  - If severe weakness or rhabdomyolysis (CK >10,000): IV fluids (0.9% NaCl, start at 200 mL/h; adjust to urine output >100 mL/h); monitor renal function
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **High-dose corticosteroids:** prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg/day) orally or IV methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily for 3 days if severe
  - **Start PPI** (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) for steroid gastroprotection
  - **Calcium + vitamin D** supplementation (if on steroids >3 months)
  - **Monitor for steroid-induced hyperglycaemia** (check capillary glucose daily)
  - **Physiotherapy** (gentle range of motion, later strengthening)
  - **Speech and language therapy** for dysphagia assessment and modified diet (e.g., soft/pureed)
  - **Screen for malignancy:** CT CAP, mammogram (if female), pelvic ultrasound (ovarian), colonoscopy if >50 or concerning symptoms
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Taper steroids** slowly (by 5 mg every 2 weeks after initial response; aim for lowest maintenance)
  - **Add steroid-sparing agent:** methotrexate (first-line, provided no ILD), azathioprine, mycophenolate, or cyclophosphamide (for severe, ILD, cardiac)
  - **IVIG** (2 g/kg over 2-5 days monthly) for refractory cases or severe dysphagia
  - **Rituximab** (off-label use) for refractory disease
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 125-150 mL/h if not NBM; adjust for rhabdomyolysis
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless bleeding risk high (consider risk vs benefit)
  - **Diet:** soft/pureed if any dysphagia; NBM if severe + SALT review
  - **Observations:** baseline then 4-hourly if stable; 1-2 hourly if on high-dose IV steroids or critically unwell; monitor for hyperglycaemia

### Drugs & Doses
- **Prednisolone:** 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO (usually 40-60 mg daily) for 4-6 weeks, then taper. IV methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg daily for 3 days if severe (e.g., ILD, dysphagia, respiratory failure).
- **Methotrexate:** start 7.5-15 mg PO/SC weekly, increase by 2.5-5 mg every 2 weeks to target 20-25 mg weekly (max 25 mg/wk); give folic acid 5 mg weekly (24h after MTX). **Check: baseline LFTs, renal function, FBC; avoid if significant ILD.**
- **Azathioprine:** 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (starting 50 mg daily, titrate up); screen for TPMT deficiency before use.
- **Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF):** 1-2 g/day in two divided doses (for moderate-to-severe disease, especially with ILD).
- **Cyclophosphamide:** IV pulsed (e.g., 500-750 mg/m² monthly) for severe ILD or life-threatening disease; must give with mesna and aggressive hydration.
- **IVIG:** 2 g/kg divided over 2-5 days monthly (infuse slowly; premedicate with antihistamines/paracetamol if needed).
- **Rituximab:** 1000 mg IV on days 1 and 15 (off-label; with pre-medication).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Methotrexate:** avoid with cotrimoxazole (increases myelotoxicity), NSAIDs (increase MTX toxicity), avoid in significant ILD, cirrhosis, uncontrolled infection
  - **Azathioprine:** avoid with allopurinol (life-threatening toxicity) — reduce azathioprine dose by 66-75% if must use allopurinol
  - **Mycophenolate:** contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic); effective contraception needed
  - **Corticosteroids:** increase risk of infection, hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, glaucoma, psychosis; taper slowly to avoid adrenal insufficiency
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Methotrexate:** contraindicated if eGFR <30; reduce dose if mild-moderate renal impairment (requires specialist advice)
  - **Azathioprine:** no major adjustment needed unless severe hepatic impairment
  - **Mycophenolate:** no adjustment needed for renal impairment
  - **Cyclophosphamide:** reduce dose in renal impairment; avoid if eGFR <20
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prednisolone:** abrupt withdrawal causing adrenal crisis — always taper (e.g., reduce by 5 mg every 2 weeks once on ≤20 mg/day; slower when ≤10 mg/day)
  - **Methotrexate:** prescribing daily instead of weekly — leads to severe myelosuppression, mucositis, hepatotoxicity; **always confirm weekly dose**
  - **Azathioprine:** starting at full dose without checking TPMT phenotype — risk of fatal myelotoxicity

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Aspiration pneumonia** from dysphagia
  - **Acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure** due to aggressive interstitial lung disease (especially anti-Jo-1)
  - **Myocarditis** (arrhythmia, heart failure)
  - **Rhabdomyolysis** (acute kidney injury)
  - **Steroid-induced:** psychosis, hyperglycaemia, upper GI bleed, fluid overload
- **Chronic:**
  - **Calcinosis cutis** (especially in juvenile DM; can ulcerate)
  - **Contractures** from prolonged weakness
  - **Malignancy** (DM more than PM; risk highest in first 3-5 years)
  - **Lipodystrophy** (associated with juvenile DM)
  - **Osteoporosis** from chronic steroids
  - **Persistent weakness** despite immunosuppression (up to 30% have incomplete recovery)

### Prognosis
- 5-year survival ~80-90% in idiopathic cases; poorer if malignancy (5-year survival ~50%), severe ILD, or cardiac involvement.
- Most patients improve with steroids, but relapses are common (30-50%); chronic immunosuppression often needed for years.
- Early detection and treatment of malignancy improves survival.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always screen for occult malignancy in DM** (CT CAP, mammogram, pelvic ultrasound, consider colonoscopy) — this is the highest-yield intervention.
- **Check PFTs and HRCT chest** in every patient with anti-Jo-1 or respiratory symptoms — ILD can progress rapidly.
- **Document baseline MRC sum score and swallow status** on admission — objective serial measurements guide treatment response.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT start steroids before checking CK, autoantibodies, and arranging muscle biopsy** (unless life-threatening) — steroids alter biopsy findings and make diagnosis impossible.
- **Do NOT use NSAIDs for myalgia** (risk of acute kidney injury in rhabdomyolysis; also interaction with methotrexate).
- **Do NOT forget PPI and calcium/vitamin D** when prescribing high-dose steroids.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - **New or worsening dysphagia** (cannot swallow saliva, drooling, coughing after eating)
  - **Shortness of breath** or new cough/fever (possible ILD or pneumonia)
  - **Muscle weakness progressing** (cannot get out of chair, tripping)
  - **New skin rash** (heliotrope, Gottron’s)
  - **Signs of infection:** fever, rigors, severe headache (especially on immunosuppression)
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Rheumatology clinic** within 2 weeks
  - **If malignancy screen pending:** oncology referral if positive; otherwise repeat screening in 6-12 months (per local guidelines)
  - **Repeat autoantibodies and CK** in 4-6 weeks to monitor response

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <90% on air, systolic BP <90) or any single parameter >3
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L**
  - **GCS ≤14** (or any drop)
  - **Acute dysphagia** (patient cannot manage own secretions — risk of aspiration)
  - **ECG shows new heart block, ventricular arrhythmia, or troponin rise**
  - **CK >10,000 U/L** with oliguria or AKI
  - **Worsening respiratory function** (fall in SpO2 >3% from baseline, need for oxygen)
- **Call ICU/anaesthetics if:**
  - **Stridor, stridor at rest, or inability to protect airway**
  - **Severe hypoxia (SpO2 <90% despite high-flow oxygen)**
  - **Cardiac arrest (obviously)**
  - **Need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation**
